name: Great Expectations
what_it_does: A Python library for defining, running, and documenting data quality Expectations (assertions) against datasets from pandas, Spark, SQL databases, and cloud storage, with automated Data Docs report generation.
use_cases: Validate that incoming data pipeline datasets conform to expected schemas, value ranges, and null constraints before processing
Run Checkpoint validations as a gate in ETL pipelines to block bad data from reaching downstream systems
Generate human-readable Data Docs reports showing data quality results for stakeholder review
Profile a new dataset automatically to generate a baseline Expectation Suite describing its statistical properties
Integrate data quality assertions into CI/CD pipelines to catch data contract violations before deployment
not_for: Real-time streaming data validation at low latency — GE is designed for batch dataset validation
Data transformation or ETL orchestration — it validates data but does not move or transform it
Simple schema-only validation where a lightweight library like Pydantic or Pandera would suffice
best_when: You need comprehensive, reusable data quality assertions with automated documentation and checkpoint-based pipeline gates for batch data workflows.
avoid_when: You need sub-second streaming validation or your data quality needs are simple enough for basic schema checks.

auth.notes: Core library requires no auth. GX Cloud (managed service) uses API keys. Data source connections use the auth mechanisms of the underlying store (database credentials, cloud IAM, etc.).

pricing.free_tier_limits: Core library is fully free and open source under Apache 2.0
pricing.paid_tiers: GX Cloud is a managed SaaS version with team collaboration features; pricing available on request
pricing.notes: The open-source library is Apache 2.0 licensed. GX Cloud adds managed infrastructure, a web UI, and team features with commercial pricing.

performance.rate_limits: No API rate limits for the open-source library; performance depends on dataset size and backend (pandas in-memory vs. SQL pushdown vs. Spark)

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Validation results are returned as structured objects with detailed failure messages per Expectation. Exception stack traces can be verbose for configuration errors. Checkpoint results include pass/fail summary with per-expectation detail.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Validations are read-only assertions; re-running the same Checkpoint against the same data produces identical results. Results are stored and can be retrieved without re-running.
known_agent_gotchas: GX 1.x (OSS) has significant breaking changes from 0.18.x — configuration files, context setup, and checkpoint APIs changed substantially; always check version in use
YAML-heavy configuration for Data Context, datasources, and Expectation Suites can be brittle — slight formatting errors cause cryptic load failures
Data Docs generation writes HTML files locally by default; in containerized or ephemeral agent environments, output paths must be explicitly configured to persistent storage
Running Expectations against SQL backends uses pushdown SQL; complex Expectations may generate inefficient queries that time out on large tables without proper indexing
Expectation Suite versioning is not built-in — if the suite changes between runs, historical validation results may no longer be comparable without external version tracking

security_score_components.security_notes: Data source credentials are managed via the Data Context configuration; secrets should be injected via environment variables. GX itself does not transmit data externally in open-source mode. GX Cloud transmits metadata but not raw data.
